input.query_title: Turing patterns in network-organized activator-inhibitor systems
input.query_abstract: Turing instability in activator-inhibitor systems provides a paradigm of nonequilibrium pattern formation; it has been extensively investigated for biological and chemical processes. Turing pattern formation should furthermore be possible in network-organized systems, such as cellular networks in morphogenesis and ecological metapopulations with dispersal connections between habitats, but investigations have so far been restricted to regular lattices and small networks. Here we report the first systematic investigation of Turing patterns in large random networks, which reveals their striking difference from the known classical behavior. In such networks, Turing instability leads to spontaneous differentiation of the network nodes into activator-rich and activator-low groups, but ordered periodic structures never develop. Only a subset of nodes having close degrees (numbers of links) undergoes differentiation, with its characteristic degree obeying a simple general law. Strong nonlinear restructuring process leads to multiple coexisting states and hysteresis effects. The final stationary patterns can be well understood in the framework of the mean-field approximation for network dynamics.
input.hyp_generic_object: coupled excitatory and inhibitory population-firing variables on a large-scale structural connectome
input.hyp_abstract: Resting-state cortical dynamics are commonly captured by coupled excitatory and inhibitory population-firing variables at each parcel, wired together through the empirical white-matter connectome. Earlier analyses emphasized regular ring or small-motif connectivity; here we characterize spontaneous spatial symmetry breaking on large, heterogeneous connectomes with realistic nodal-degree distributions. Linearizing the neural-mass equations about the spatially uniform low-firing equilibrium and expanding perturbations in the eigenbasis of the connectome Laplacian yields a discrete dispersion relation, the graph analogue of a continuous wavenumber spectrum. When the effective long-range spread of the inhibitory field across association fibers sufficiently exceeds that of the recurrent excitatory drive, the quiescent equilibrium, though stable to uniform fluctuations, is destabilized along specific Laplacian eigenmodes. Rather than periodic cortical stripes, the network partitions into persistently hyperactive and hypoactive regions; only parcels whose nodal connectivity falls near a critical value participate in the split, and we derive a closed-form expression for that critical connectivity. Saturating firing-rate nonlinearities generate several coexisting activity configurations and history-dependent switching between them under slowly ramped global drive. A mean-field reduction over connectivity classes reproduces the multistable repertoire, indicating a purely topological route by which connectome heterogeneity organizes large-scale functional differentiation without spatial periodicity, with implications for interpreting individual variability in resting-state activity amplitude across cortical areas.
input.candidate_title: Ising Model on Networks with an Arbitrary Distribution of Connections
input.candidate_abstract: We find the exact critical temperature $T_c$ of the nearest-neighbor ferromagnetic Ising model on an `equilibrium' random graph with an arbitrary degree distribution $P(k)$. We observe an anomalous behavior of the magnetization, magnetic susceptibility and specific heat, when $P(k)$ is fat-tailed, or, loosely speaking, when the fourth moment of the distribution diverges in infinite networks. When the second moment becomes divergent, $T_c$ approaches infinity, the phase transition is of infinite order, and size effect is anomalously strong.
